summary: Drew DeVault recounts attending XDC 2018 in A Coruña, Spain, alongside four other major wlroots contributors. The team learned from kernel and mesa driver developers, discovered bugs in their DRM code, and began onboarding new contributors. He had a notable encounter with Nvidia representatives who already knew him from his critical writings, concluding that Nvidia's engineering culture remains an obstacle to open source participation. While Wayland protocol standardization discussions were limited due to absent attendees, the conference was productive for cross-project collaboration and knowledge sharing.
key_points: The wlroots team discovered bugs in their DRM code through conversations with kernel developers and began onboarding new contributors from the conference
Intel's new CI platform, which tests code on every generation of Intel GPU in about 30 minutes, inspired plans for builds.sr.ht features
Despite a productive conversation with Nvidia engineers, DeVault concluded that Nvidia's unhealthy engineering culture prevents meaningful open source involvement
Wayland protocol standardization discussions were limited due to key community members being absent
Cross-pollination between projects was a major outcome, with wlroots developers becoming contributors to other projects and vice versa
money_quotes: wlroots was among the highest-level software represented at XDC. Most of the attendees are hacking on the kernel or mesa drivers, and we had a lot to learn from each other.

On the night of the kick-off party, I led the wlroots clan to the bar for beers and introduced myself to the people who were standing there - who already knew me from my writings critical of Nvidia. Awkward!
I was sad to conclude that we still aren't going to see any meaningful involvement in open source from Nvidia.
Many of their engineers are open to it, but I think that the engineering culture at Nvidia is unhealthy and that the engineers have very little influence.
We made our case and brought up points they weren't thinking about, and I can only hope they'll take them home and work on gradually improving the culture.
We found new contributors to our projects, and have been made into new contributors for everyone else's projects.
key_insight: In-person developer conferences create invaluable cross-project collaboration and knowledge transfer, but systemic corporate culture problems like Nvidia's remain resistant to even direct, good-faith engagement from the open source community.
